subject: Forged identity card from World War II, Athens, used by Greek-Jewish woman Eva Alhanati under the false Christian name 'Evangelia Alexiou' to survive the Axis occupation
context_for_lesson: An actual WWII forged identity card from German-occupied Athens, used by Eva Alhanati, a Greek-Jewish woman who survived the Holocaust by living under the Christian alias 'Evangelia Alexiou'. Like the Wiernik Kennkarte, this is a real example of the kind of document that saved lives. For Judy Abrams's story, it is one more way to physically illustrate what 'false papers' meant: a piece of cardboard with a different name and a different religion that could mean the difference between life and death. Pairing this with the Wiernik card from Poland and Judy's own story from Hungary lets students see that hidden-Jewish-child stories happened across all of Nazi-occupied Europe.
author: TheRamtzi (Wikimedia uploader; the original document was issued during WWII in Athens)
date_taken: Document is from WWII (1941-1944 Athens occupation period); image uploaded 16 November 2013
license_summary: CC BY-SA 3.0 — free to use, share, and adapt with attribution; derivative works must use the same license
attribution_required: Photo: TheRamtzi via Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA 3.0)
lesson_use_intent: Optional supplement to the Wiernik Kennkarte on page 3 (Before We Read), OR can substitute for it. Caption: 'A real WWII forged identity card. This one belonged to Eva Alhanati, a Greek-Jewish woman who survived by living as the Christian 'Evangelia Alexiou'. Judy's papers identified her as Ilona, a Catholic peasant girl. False papers like these existed all across Nazi-occupied Europe.'
concerns: Greek, not Hungarian — caption must clarify this. Similar resolution constraint to the Wiernik card (medium-size on page, not full-page feature). One of these two forged-ID cards is sufficient pedagogically — Brandon can choose which fits better with his page layout and which story serves the lesson better.
